title: The ethics of remembering someone better than they were
summary: Argue that the common practice of idealising the dead — softening their faults in memory and in speech — is neither simply dishonest nor simply kind, but raises genuine moral questions about the obligations of the living to the dead, to the truth, and to those still living.
topic: The moral complexity of idealising the dead in memory and speech
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay exploring the ethics of remembering someone — a relative, a public figure, a colleague — better than they actually were. You may distinguish between private memory and public commemoration, and between the interests of different parties: the dead, the living, and historical truth. Deploy inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid sentimentality. Treat the question as a genuine moral problem with competing claims.
hintStarters: Rarely does the moment of a person's death settle the question of who they were — if anything, it reopens it…
What makes the idealisation of the dead more than a harmless kindness is the existence of…
Granted, the dead cannot be harmed by misrepresentation in the way the living can. Even so,…

trí nhớ phản thực, ký ức về điều lẽ ra đã có thể xảy ra
sự xét lại tại giường bệnh, thay đổi đánh giá khi ai đó sắp chết
người thân xa cách
khả năng tiếp cận nhận thức, quyền biết sự thật
người hưởng lợi từ sự im lặng
gánh nặng đạo đức của sự sống sót
sự tưởng niệm công cộng
sau khi chết

sampleText:
Rarely does the moment of a person's death settle the question of who they were — if anything, it reopens it. The eulogy, the obituary, the family gathering around a table where a difficult person's name is now safe to say kindly: these are not neutral acts of remembrance. They are negotiations, conducted under the pressure of grief, propriety, and the presence of others who knew the same person differently. The question of whether to say what one actually remembers, or to say something kinder and less complete, is one that most people face, and almost none examine.

The case for charitable memory is not trivial. Grief is not a courtroom; the dead cannot defend themselves, clarify a misunderstood action, or contextualise a failure that looks different from the outside than it felt from the inside. A person who was difficult in middle age may have been shaped by circumstances that the bereaved neither knew nor were owed. There is something epistemically honest about the recognition that one's account of another person is always partial, and that death forecloses the possibility of the kind of revision that longer acquaintance might have permitted.

Granted, the dead cannot be harmed by misrepresentation in the way the living can. Even so, the practice of remembering the dead better than they were does not affect only the dead. It affects the living — and in some cases, the living who were most directly harmed by the person being remembered. The child of an abusive parent who watches that parent's eulogy transform them into a figure of warmth and wisdom has not simply been asked to tolerate a social convention. They have been asked to participate in a narrative that renders their own experience unintelligible — that makes the harm they suffered a kind of categorical error, a misreading of a person who was, apparently, nothing like what they experienced.

This is what the ethics literature sometimes calls collateral silencing: when the idealisation of one person structurally forecloses the testimony of another. The survivor of a difficult or harmful relationship is not simply omitted from the commemorative narrative; they are implicitly contradicted by it. Their account, if offered, would constitute an impiety — a violation of the commemorative norm that protects the dead from the judgements of the living. It is this asymmetry — the dead protected, the living silenced — that makes the ethics of posthumous idealisation more than a question of private sentiment.

The distinction between private memory and public commemoration matters here. One may, without dishonesty, hold a complicated private memory — acknowledging what was difficult while also attending to what was genuine — without importing that complexity into every public statement. The choice not to enumerate a parent's failures at a funeral is not the same as the choice to celebrate a colonial administrator as a humanitarian on a public monument. The first is a concession to the specific conditions of grief and communal gathering; the second is a historical claim that shapes how future generations understand the past, and that actively marginalises those whose historical experience the celebration distorts.

What the ethics of memory requires is not the abolition of charitable remembrance, but the cultivation of what one might call calibrated honesty — an account that does not compress a person into their worst moments, but does not erase the harm they caused from the record either. It is the erasure — the hagiography that converts a flawed person into an unambiguous saint — that does the deepest moral work, because it instructs those who were harmed by that person that their harm was either imagined or deserved. The living owe something to the dead, but they owe something to each other too, and those obligations do not always point in the same direction.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after a negative adverb to open with moral uncertainty
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Rarely does the moment of a person's death settle the question of who they were' đảo trợ động từ 'does' trước chủ ngữ 'the moment' sau trạng từ phủ định 'Rarely'. Cấu trúc đảo ngữ này tạo sức nặng tức thì và đặt câu hỏi trung tâm — về bản sắc và ký ức — ngay từ đầu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Rarely does the moment of a person's death settle the question of who they were' inverts auxiliary 'does' before subject 'the moment' after negative adverb 'Rarely' — opening with immediate moral weight. The conditional extension 'if anything, it reopens it' deepens the paradox at the sentence's close, a C1 technique.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to isolate the primary mechanism of harm
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is this asymmetry — the dead protected, the living silenced — that makes the ethics of posthumous idealisation more than a question of private sentiment.' dùng 'It is X that Y' để cô lập sự bất đối xứng cụ thể. Hai cụm apposition ngắn gọn 'the dead protected, the living silenced' trong dấu gạch ngang là ví dụ điển hình của câu văn C1 nén chặt.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is this asymmetry — the dead protected, the living silenced — that makes the ethics of posthumous idealisation more than a question of private sentiment.' uses 'It is X that Y' to isolate the asymmetry as the key ethical issue. The two compressed appositives in em-dashes ('the dead protected, the living silenced') are a C1 technique: dense, parallel, and structurally elegant.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hiếm khi khoảnh khắc một người qua đời giải quyết câu hỏi về họ là ai — nếu có, nó mở lại câu hỏi đó. Điếu văn, cáo phó, gia đình quây quần quanh bàn nơi tên của một người khó tính giờ đây có thể được nhắc đến một cách tử tế: đây không phải là những hành động tưởng niệm trung lập. Chúng là những cuộc thương lượng, được tiến hành dưới áp lực của nỗi đau, lễ nghi và sự hiện diện của những người biết cùng một người theo cách khác nhau.

Lập luận ủng hộ ký ức từ bi không phải là không đáng kể. Nỗi đau không phải là toà án; người chết không thể tự bào chữa. Cái chết đóng lại khả năng của loại xét lại mà quen biết lâu hơn có thể đã cho phép.

Cứ cho là người chết không thể bị tổn hại bởi sự xuyên tạc theo cách người sống có thể. Dù vậy, thực hành nhớ người chết tốt hơn thực tế không chỉ ảnh hưởng đến người chết. Đứa trẻ của một người cha mẹ lạm dụng xem điếu văn biến cha mẹ họ thành hình tượng ấm áp và khôn ngoan đã không chỉ bị yêu cầu chịu đựng một quy ước xã hội. Họ bị yêu cầu tham gia vào một câu chuyện làm cho trải nghiệm của chính họ trở nên không thể hiểu được.

Đây là điều mà văn học đạo đức đôi khi gọi là sự im lặng thứ cấp: khi sự lý tưởng hoá của một người về mặt cấu trúc đóng lại lời chứng của người khác. Chính sự bất đối xứng — người chết được bảo vệ, người sống bị im lặng — mới làm cho đạo đức của sự lý tưởng hoá sau khi chết nhiều hơn một câu hỏi về tình cảm cá nhân.

Điều mà đạo đức ký ức đòi hỏi không phải là sự bãi bỏ ký ức từ bi, mà là sự trồng trọt sự thành thật được hiệu chỉnh. Người sống nợ người chết điều gì đó, nhưng họ cũng nợ nhau, và những nghĩa vụ đó không phải lúc nào cũng chỉ cùng một hướng.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses inversion after a negative adverb?
options: Rarely the moment of a person's death settles the question of who they were.
Rarely does the moment of a person's death settle the question of who they were.
The moment of death rarely does settle who they were.
Rarely, a person's death does not settle who they were.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Rarely', auxiliary 'does' must precede subject 'the moment' — standard subject-auxiliary inversion after a negative fronted adverb.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay describe as 'negotiations' in the context of remembering the dead?
options: Legal disputes over the estate of a deceased person
The eulogy, obituary, and family gatherings — acts of remembrance conducted under pressure of grief, propriety, and different accounts of the same person
Formal mediation sessions between estranged family members
Diplomatic efforts to protect the reputation of a public figure
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay calls the eulogy, obituary, and family gathering 'negotiations, conducted under the pressure of grief, propriety, and the presence of others who knew the same person differently'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at what the essay lists as examples of 'negotiations' in the first paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's argument in favour of charitable memory?
options: The dead deserve to be remembered positively as a universal moral principle
One's knowledge of any person is partial, and death forecloses the revision that longer acquaintance might have permitted
Criticising the dead is always a form of self-promotion
Grief prevents accurate memory, so charitable accounts are more honest
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues 'one's account of another person is always partial, and that death forecloses the possibility of the kind of revision that longer acquaintance might have permitted' — an epistemic argument for charity.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph beginning 'The case for charitable memory is not trivial.'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'collateral silencing' mean in the essay?
options: When a legal order prevents someone from speaking
When the idealisation of a dead person structurally forecloses the testimony of someone harmed by that person
When a group of people collectively decide not to speak about a difficult subject
When background noise prevents a conversation from being heard
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines it as what happens 'when the idealisation of one person structurally forecloses the testimony of another' — the survivor is implicitly contradicted by the celebratory narrative.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph beginning 'This is what the ethics literature sometimes calls collateral silencing'.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay distinguish between a private funeral eulogy and a public monument?
options: Private eulogies are for family; public monuments are for strangers.
A funeral eulogy is a concession to grief and communal conditions; a public monument makes a historical claim that shapes future understanding and marginalises those the celebration distorts.
Private memory is always less accurate than public commemoration.
Public monuments are subject to democratic revision; private eulogies are permanent.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues the funeral eulogy is 'a concession to the specific conditions of grief and communal gathering' while the public monument is 'a historical claim that shapes how future generations understand the past'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph beginning 'The distinction between private memory and public commemoration matters here.'

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to isolate the key ethical asymmetry?
options: This asymmetry — the dead protected, the living silenced — makes the ethics more than private sentiment.
It is this asymmetry — the dead protected, the living silenced — that makes the ethics of posthumous idealisation more than a question of private sentiment.
What makes the ethics a public question is the asymmetry between dead and living.
The asymmetry between the dead and the living is what makes this an ethical issue.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X that Y' cleft isolates the asymmetry as the decisive factor; the em-dash appositives 'the dead protected, the living silenced' add compressed structural elegance.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is … that …' structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'hagiography' mean in the essay?
options: A form of religious prayer recited at funerals
Uncritical biography or remembrance that converts a flawed person into an unambiguous saint
A legal document protecting a person's posthumous reputation
The study of ancient written records
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'hagiography' to mean the practice that 'converts a flawed person into an unambiguous saint' — originally the biography of a saint, now used to describe any excessively flattering account.
mcQuestions.hint: Hagiography originally meant the biography of a Christian saint — what does that imply about its current figurative use?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay propose as an alternative to both idealisation and harsh retrospective judgement?
options: A formal process of posthumous review conducted by ethics committees
Calibrated honesty — an account that neither compresses a person into their worst moments nor erases the harm they caused
A waiting period of ten years before any public commemoration
Allowing only those who knew the person to speak at commemorative events
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay proposes 'calibrated honesty — an account that does not compress a person into their worst moments, but does not erase the harm they caused from the record either.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph beginning 'What the ethics of memory requires…'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about a survivor who offers a contradictory account of a deceased person at a commemoration?
options: They are behaving appropriately by insisting on the truth
Their account 'would constitute an impiety — a violation of the commemorative norm'
They are legally protected in most Western democracies
They would be supported by the ethics of testimonial justice
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the survivor's account, 'if offered, would constitute an impiety — a violation of the commemorative norm that protects the dead from the judgements of the living.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'impiety' and 'commemorative norm' near the discussion of the survivor.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition correctly signals a concession followed by a counter-argument?
options: Even so, the dead cannot be harmed by misrepresentation. Granted, it affects the living.
Granted, the dead cannot be harmed by misrepresentation in the way the living can. Even so, the practice affects the living — and particularly those harmed by the person being remembered.
Furthermore, the dead cannot be harmed; in addition, the living are affected.
Although the dead cannot be harmed, granted this affects the living.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted… Even so…' is the canonical concede-and-counter structure; the concession acknowledges the opposing point before the pivot reasserts.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'the harm they suffered was a kind of categorical error'?
options: The harm was caused by an administrative mistake
The idealised narrative implies the survivor misclassified their experience — the harmed person made an error by categorising kindness as abuse
The harm belonged to a category that should not be discussed publicly
The harm was so serious it constituted a different order of wrong
mcQuestions.explanation: If the deceased was 'apparently nothing like what they experienced', then the survivor's experience of harm is implicitly framed as a misreading — a categorical mistake — not a genuine injury.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what a 'categorical error' implies about the validity of an interpretation.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'testimonial injustice' describe in the context of the essay?
options: A legal failure to call witnesses in a court case
The situation in which a person's testimony or account is implicitly discredited — here, by a commemorative narrative that contradicts it
Injustice caused by inaccurate historical records
The process of gathering testimonials for a posthumous award
mcQuestions.explanation: Testimonial injustice occurs when a person's credibility as a witness is undermined — here, the survivor's account is implicitly invalidated by the idealised narrative of the deceased.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about whose account is being trusted and whose is being discredited.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay argues the living owe obligations both to the dead and to each other. What does it say about these obligations?
options: Obligations to the living always outweigh obligations to the dead
Obligations to the dead always outweigh obligations to the living
These obligations do not always point in the same direction and must be weighed against each other
The obligations are identical and can be met by a single standard of honesty
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's final sentence: 'The living owe something to the dead, but they owe something to each other too, and those obligations do not always point in the same direction.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the essay's final sentence.

options: The reputation a person builds before their death
How a person is remembered and regarded after their death
A legal protection for the names of deceased public figures
The digital footprint a person leaves behind after death
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Posthumous' means occurring after death — posthumous reputation is the standing and assessment a person receives once they can no longer influence or defend it.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Post-' means after; 'humous' relates to the ground/burial — posthumous means after death.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay say 'the living cannot defend themselves' is NOT the reason for charitable memory — and what does it say instead?
options: The living can always defend themselves through legal action
Charitable memory is justified not by the dead's defencelessness but by the epistemically honest recognition that one's account of any person is always partial
The dead should be defended by their families, who knew them best
Charitable memory is a social convention that requires no moral justification
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues for charitable memory on epistemic grounds: 'one's account of another person is always partial' — not merely because the dead can't respond, but because all human knowledge of others is incomplete.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the epistemic argument in the paragraph beginning 'The case for charitable memory is not trivial.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever attended a funeral, memorial, or commemorative event where the account given of the deceased seemed incomplete, idealised, or in tension with your own knowledge of them? What did you do with that tension? What do you think the people present owed to the deceased — and to each other?
